full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> It was late spring in 2009, and my family was in Washington, D.C.,<|8.08|> <|8.08|> to witness my cousin's graduation from Georgetown University.<|13.88|> <|13.88|> It was Sunday morning, and I'd never been to the Cathedral Church of St. Peter and St. Paul,<|22.56|> <|22.56|> and so I walked the few blocks it took to get there for worship.<|27.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.14|> If you are ever in our nation's capital,<|3.64|> <|3.64|> I strongly recommend making a pilgrimage<|7.94|> <|7.94|> to Washington National Cathedral,<|10.62|> <|10.62|> as the cathedral is better known.<|14.04|> <|14.04|> It is a feast for the senses,<|16.72|> <|16.72|> architecturally and musically, yes,<|19.82|> <|19.82|> and on May 17th, 2009, it was a homiletical feast.<|26.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> a feast of preaching, a feast of words.<|5.00|> <|5.00|> I can't help but reflect on all that has changed internationally,<|12.00|> <|12.00|> nationally, locally, and personally since that time.<|18.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In 2009, Sam Lloyd was still Dean of the National Cathedral.<|8.00|> <|8.00|> iPhones were only two years old.<|12.00|> <|12.00|> The climate crisis was still being described by many as decades away.<|19.00|> <|19.00|> And Uber was in its second month of operation.<|24.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> A different time, amen? And even though I have changed tremendously in the 13 intervening years, I read Sam's sermon at least annually as a sort of touchstone of my own journey.<|18.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In the sermon, Dean Lloyd explores God's most fundamental character, love, through the lens of the airport terminal scene of the 2003 Christmas rom-com, Love Actually.<|17.58|> <|17.58|> Love.<|18.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> One of the characters narrates the scene by saying that whenever he gets gloomy about the state of the world,<|9.00|> <|9.00|> he thinks about the arrivals terminal at Heathrow Airport and the uncomplicated delight you see on the faces of friends and family as they find each other and reconnect.<|23.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Then he goes on to say that all the known parting messages by people who died on 9-11 were messages of love.<|10.70|> <|12.28|> And at the end, the film closes with another scene of airport greetings.<|17.84|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> things.<|1.36|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Sam then takes and makes a connection that I'll never forget.<|5.46|> <|6.60|> The gospel readings we hear from John in the great 50 days of Easter<|11.68|> <|11.68|> called the farewell discourse by scholars<|15.52|> <|15.52|> are Jesus's final words to his disciples and by extension us<|23.08|> <|23.08|> before his death, resurrection, and ascension.<|29.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> His final important words before his cosmic flight departs, if you will.<|7.06|> <|9.08|> Jesus distills for the apostles the nugget at the heart of life with God.<|16.18|> <|17.40|> Love.<|18.26|> <|19.62|> Love.<|20.22|> <|20.22|> Love.<|21.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> just as I have loved you, you also should love one another.<|6.52|> <|7.90|> Just as I have loved you, exactly as I have loved you,<|12.62|> <|12.94|> the manner in which I have loved you, in the same way I have loved you,<|18.18|> <|19.30|> love one another."<|21.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> When Christians hear Jesus use phrases like,<|4.40|> <|4.40|> just as, or in the same way, or exactly as,<|11.04|> <|12.32|> our spidey senses tingle.<|14.44|> <|16.14|> What specifically is Jesus referring to when he says,<|21.64|> <|22.28|> just as?<|23.98|> <|23.98|> Just as.<|25.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In John 13, Jesus is at dinner at the Last Supper with his friends.<|8.58|> <|9.62|> Then he gets up from dinner, takes off his outer robe, ties a towel around himself, pours water into a basin, and begins to wash the disciples' feet and to wipe them with a towel that was tied around him.<|28.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This is the just as of which Jesus speaks.<|7.80|> <|9.40|> It is not the newest book.<|11.44|> <|11.64|> It is not the newest program.<|13.08|> <|13.48|> It is not the newest trend.<|15.12|> <|15.12|> It is not the newest QVC deal.<|18.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> No, it is the new but very ancient commandment.<|4.62|> <|5.18|> Love one another just as I have loved you,<|10.92|> <|11.14|> just as I have knelt at your feet and washed them.<|15.32|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus, like any person conscious of their impending death,<|6.80|> <|6.80|> is sharing his heart's most urgent message before it is too late.<|13.24|> <|13.24|> One of the challenges of this public health crisis<|18.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> is that it makes outward and visible the inward invisible reality<|6.64|> <|6.64|> that life is fragile and finite,<|10.72|> <|11.62|> even for those who are not terminally ill.<|15.72|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus, knowing the forces of death were conspiring against him, kneels, wraps a towel around his waist, and begins washing and wiping the feet of his small circle of disciples.<|14.66|> <|14.66|> Jesus,<|15.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Instead of offering another parable or a new teaching,<|4.84|> <|5.40|> Jesus lives his greatest parable by practicing deep incarnation with those he loves.<|13.22|> <|14.68|> In the words of one scholar, Jesus told so many parables that he became a parable,<|20.54|> <|21.14|> ate so many meals with outcasts and sinners that he became a meal for us.<|27.06|> <|27.06|> Amen.<|28.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> washing the feet of his loved ones and sharing with them the urgency of love before his crucifixion.<|9.00|> <|9.00|> This is the love of God in Christ in elegant, if messy, action.<|17.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> For Christians, love is more than just an idea.<|6.06|> <|7.38|> For Christians, those who have found themselves loved and treasured by God<|13.56|> <|13.56|> and waiting in the waters of baptism,<|16.06|> <|17.06|> love is chemo visits and panicked treks to the pediatrician.<|24.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Love is holding the hand of a loved one in hospice.<|6.00|> <|6.00|> Love is showing up to couples therapy when your marriage vows are strained by the weight of busyness and conflict.<|18.00|> <|18.00|> Love is staying up that extra hour to listen to that friend who can't seem to take a break and catch a break.<|28.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Love is a verb. Love does.<|6.44|> <|6.44|> Love is the reproductive health doctor who helps her patients navigate the<|13.68|> <|13.68|> cumulative barriers to safe and accessible reproductive health care.<|20.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Love is the parent who leaves the encouraging note in their child's lunchbox.<|8.50|> <|8.50|> Love is a verb.<|12.00|> <|12.00|> It's more than an idea.<|15.00|> <|15.00|> Love does.<|18.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And in every loving act, every compassionate gesture, every person able to rest a little<|10.50|> <|10.50|> bit better because their rights aren't walking a threadbare tightrope, Jesus is present,<|18.66|> <|19.88|> cupping ever gingerly our heels, saying, give me the other one.<|27.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|1.38|><|endoftext|>
